finding: an individual attribute (body mass index) and attitudes correlated with an approach bias in motor action toward palatable stimuli
frequent use of a platform was associated with stronger spontaneous hedonic reactions to platform cues, which partially mediated the relationship between use frequency and reported cravings
people resisted certain imaginative activities; genre was identified as a significant factor explaining this resistance
people's ordinary-language use of 'intentional' to describe behaviors did not align with philosophical criteria for intentionality
married men's performance of female-typed household tasks showed a systematic hierarchical ordering obscured by conventional measurement approaches

poor economic performance decreased positive framing and increased negative framing of an institution; high unemployment more strongly affected framing among high-educated individuals, while GDP growth more strongly affected low-educated individuals

higher parental socioeconomic status delayed entry into first union; this effect was stronger for direct marriage than cohabitation, and cross-national variation was explained by educational attainment
older adults showed reduced prosocial behavior toward nonrelatives but similar or greater behavior toward relatives compared to younger adults; older adults displayed stronger kinship bias
a brief intervention incorporating proactive coping increased participants' proactive skills, goal achievement, and self-efficacy compared to control
viewing multiple identical product units as a group (versus a single unit) increased perceived product efficacy through increased perception of product homogeneity
perception of unrelated goal conflict increased time spent on justifiable activities and decreased time on less-justifiable activities
feeling financially constrained increased preference for durable material goods over experiences through heightened concern about lasting utility
poor economic conditions biased parental spending toward female offspring through increased perceived relative reproductive value of females
angular versus circular logo shapes influenced brand attribute judgments through activation of hardness versus softness associations, mediated by imagery generation in working memory
salience of resource scarcity enhanced product-use creativity through activation of a constraint mindset that reduced functional fixedness
displaying healthy items to the left versus right of unhealthy items increased healthy choice through congruence with mental organization, enhancing self-control
exposure to limited-quantity promotion advertising activated actual aggression through perception of social competition and physiological arousal
color saturation increased perceived product size through attention capture and arousal; downstream effects included higher willingness-to-pay when saturation matched usage goals

polarizing products (bimodal rating distributions) were perceived as more self-expressive and more desirable among individuals with low self-concept clarity or high motivation for individual identity expression

perceived financial well-being (current money management stress and expected future financial security) predicted overall well-being comparably to other life domains; relative importance varied by income group
